eli3: Bayesian inference is the process of updating beliefs about unknown quantities using data via Bayes' theorem. Start with a prior P(θ), observe data x, compute the posterior P(θ|x) ∝ P(x|θ)P(θ). All inference — point estimates, intervals, predictions, model comparison — flows from the posterior.

The posterior predictive distribution P(x̃|x) = ∫ P(x̃|θ)P(θ|x)dθ predicts new observations x̃ by averaging the likelihood over the posterior. This automatically propagates parameter uncertainty into predictions — unlike plug-in methods that substitute a point estimate for θ and ignore estimation uncertainty.

Bayesian model comparison uses the Bayes factor BF₁₂ = P(x|M₁)/P(x|M₂), where each marginal likelihood integrates out parameters. Unlike p-values, Bayes factors directly quantify evidence for one model over another, can support H₀ (BF < 1/3 is evidence for H₀), and penalize model complexity automatically via Occam's razor.

whenWhy.title: Full uncertainty propagation into predictions
whenWhy.body: When downstream decisions depend on predictions, propagating parameter uncertainty matters. The posterior predictive P(x̃|x) = ∫P(x̃|θ)P(θ|x)dθ averages over all plausible parameter values, producing calibrated prediction uncertainty. Plug-in estimates (using θ̂_MLE) underestimate predictive uncertainty, especially in small samples.

whenWhy.title: Hierarchical models for grouped data
whenWhy.body: Bayesian hierarchical (multilevel) models estimate group-specific parameters while sharing information across groups via a common prior — partial pooling. For K groups with sparse data per group, complete pooling (one global estimate) ignores group differences; no pooling (separate estimates) overfits. Partial pooling shrinks group estimates toward the grand mean, reducing MSE. This is Stein's phenomenon rediscovered in a Bayesian framework.

whenWhy.title: Bayesian model selection and averaging
whenWhy.body: The Bayes factor BF₁₂ = P(x|M₁)/P(x|M₂) compares models. Jeffreys' scale: BF > 100 (decisive evidence for M₁), 10–100 (strong), 3–10 (moderate), 1–3 (anecdotal). Bayesian model averaging (BMA) weights predictions from multiple models by their posterior model probabilities, avoiding the need to select a single model.

whenWhy.title: Decision-making under uncertainty
whenWhy.body: In Bayesian decision theory, the optimal action a* minimizes expected loss: a* = argmin_a ∫ L(a,θ)P(θ|x)dθ. The posterior replaces the unknown distribution with a probability-weighted average over plausible values. This framework unifies inference and decision-making and is the basis for medical decision analysis, reinforcement learning (POMDP), and bandit algorithms.

deepDiveIntro: The posterior combines the likelihood of the data given your parameters with your prior beliefs, then normalises by the total probability of the data. Computing that normalising constant requires integrating over all possible parameter values — which is usually analytically intractable. In practice you use MCMC (which samples the posterior exactly in the long run), variational Bayes (faster but approximate), or a Laplace approximation (treating the posterior as a bell curve centred on its peak).

A key theoretical result: with enough data, the posterior becomes approximately normal regardless of what prior you started with, concentrating tightly around the true parameter value. This means frequentist and Bayesian inference give the same answers for large samples.

deepDive.heading: Posterior Predictive Distribution
deepDive.body: To predict new data, Bayesian inference averages the prediction over all plausible parameter values, weighted by how likely each is given the data you have seen. This produces wider prediction intervals than simply plugging in a point estimate, because it honestly accounts for uncertainty about the parameters themselves — not just uncertainty in the data. The extra spread in predictions is a feature, not a bug. [1]
deepDive.formula: P(x̃ | x) = ∫ P(x̃ | θ) P(θ | x) dθ

deepDive.heading: Marginal Likelihood and Occam's Razor
deepDive.body: The marginal likelihood measures how well a model predicts the data on average, integrating over all possible parameter values. A complex model with many free parameters spreads its prior probability mass very thin across a huge space; most of those configurations predict the data poorly. A simpler, well-specified model concentrates its prior on fewer configurations and thus achieves a higher average likelihood. This is Bayesian Occam's razor: complexity is automatically penalised without any explicit regularisation term. [1]

deepDive.heading: Laplace Approximation
deepDive.body: Rather than running full MCMC, the Laplace approximation fits a bell curve to the posterior around its peak. You find the peak (the MAP estimate), then measure how curved the posterior is at that point — a steeper curve means a narrower bell. This gives a Gaussian approximation to the full posterior that is fast to compute and enables uncertainty estimates without sampling. Setting a flat prior in this framework gives the BIC model selection criterion. [1]

deepDive.heading: Hierarchical Bayesian Models
deepDive.body: Hierarchical models treat group-level parameters as drawn from a shared distribution with its own parameters learned from the data. Groups with a lot of data trust their own observations; groups with sparse data borrow strength from the others and are pulled toward the overall average. This partial pooling consistently reduces estimation error compared to either treating all groups as identical or fitting each group completely independently. [1]

takeaways: Posterior P(θ|x) ∝ P(x|θ)P(θ); inference (estimates, intervals, predictions) flows from the posterior. [1]
Posterior predictive P(x̃|x) = ∫P(x̃|θ)P(θ|x)dθ propagates parameter uncertainty into predictions. [1]
BvM theorem: posterior → N(θ₀, I(θ₀)⁻¹/n) for large n, regardless of prior. [1]
Bayes factor = P(x|M₁)/P(x|M₂): direct model comparison; automatically penalizes complexity. [1]
Hierarchical models achieve partial pooling, shrinking group estimates toward a common mean. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does the BvM theorem fail?
edgeCases.a: BvM fails when: (1) the model is misspecified (the true distribution is not in the model family); (2) the parameter is infinite-dimensional (nonparametric models); (3) the parameter is on the boundary of the parameter space; (4) the parameter is not identifiable. In misspecified models, the posterior concentrates at the parameter minimizing KL divergence to the true distribution, not at the true parameter — valid but not the data-generating parameter. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is variational Bayes and when is it preferred to MCMC?
edgeCases.a: Variational Bayes (VB) approximates the posterior by minimizing KL(q(θ) || P(θ|x)) over a tractable family q (e.g., mean-field: q(θ) = ∏ qᵢ(θᵢ)). VB is much faster than MCMC for large datasets and high-dimensional models (deep learning, large-scale topic models) but underestimates posterior variance (due to the forward KL). MCMC provides asymptotically exact samples but is slower. Use VB for exploration/scale; MCMC for accurate posterior characterization. [1]

sources.label: Gelman et al. — Bayesian Data Analysis (3rd ed.), Ch. 4, 6
Bishop — Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning, Ch. 10
